pattern: Peeling equal matching pieces from both ends greedily maximizes the chunk count, because committing to the shortest balanced prefix-suffix pair never blocks a better split.
hints: The first and last chunks must be identical strings, so look for matching pieces growing inward from both ends.
Greedily take the shortest prefix that equals the corresponding suffix; that adds two chunks and recurses on the middle.
Walk two pointers inward, comparing a growing prefix against an equal-length suffix; on a match, count two and restart on the remaining inner segment, counting one if a leftover middle survives.

edge_inputs.description: no split possible, whole string is one chunk
all same character splits to length
odd-length all-same leaves a middle chunk

stress.note: tiny alphabet produces frequent prefix/suffix matches

solutions.python:
class Solution:
    def longestDecomposition(self, text):
        count = 0
        i, j = 0, len(text) - 1
        l = 0
        prefix = ''
        suffix = ''
        # two-pointer greedy from outside in
        lo, hi = 0, len(text)
        while lo < hi:
            k = 1
            matched = False
            while lo + k <= hi - k:
                if text[lo:lo + k] == text[hi - k:hi]:
                    count += 2
                    lo += k
                    hi -= k
                    matched = True
                    break
                k += 1
            if not matched:
                count += 1
                break
        return count
